w:r.w:t: POC complete (both tests passing); Electron app scaffold is the next step
brainstorm-session-2026-05-15.md · adr-001-electron-framework.md · CLAUDE.md · README.md (POC) · Momentum Log Templates.xlsx

Momentum is an empathy-oriented Windows desktop app that watches active app and website usage, detects distraction, and responds with CBT-based interventions — treating procrastination as an emotional regulation problem rather than a time-management one.

Many apps and websites are deliberately engineered to capture and retain attention. For the people Momentum serves, that design drives phone and internet addiction, reinforces procrastination feedback loops, and erodes work habits. The harm is most acute for remote workers and neurodiverse individuals — particularly those with ADHD — who lack the ambient structure of an office and are more susceptible to attention capture.

A large share of younger knowledge workers will openly admit they spend far too much time on their devices unproductively, dislike that they are doing it, and feel unable to stop. Existing tools do not solve this. Site and app blockers are passive and treat the symptom, not the root cause. Task managers and time-management apps track and remind, but none actively watch what the user is doing and intervene in the moment.
The founder's own experience defines the core insight:
for overcoming procrastination — but they are abandoned after 2–4 weeks without active, daily support. The thesis behind Momentum is that addiction-driven procrastination is best addressed as an
, and that constant, in-the-moment support is what turns a short-lived CBT habit into a lasting one.
“Procrastination isn’t a time management problem, it’s an emotional regulation problem.”

Cost of not solving it: the user continues a cycle of avoidance and guilt, CBT techniques that demonstrably help go unused, and the productivity tools already on the market leave the underlying emotional driver untouched.
Momentum V1 is built for and validated against a single, well-understood primary user. Broader segmentation is deferred until the core loop is proven.
Primary persona — the distraction-prone knowledge worker
Works remotely or independently, without the ambient accountability of a shared workspace.
Is neurodiverse or otherwise prone to distraction (ADHD is the archetype), and experiences procrastination as an emotional spiral, not a scheduling gap.
Has tried CBT-style techniques (thought records, reframing) and found them effective — but cannot sustain them unaided beyond a few weeks.
Spends meaningful unproductive time on attention-capturing apps and sites, is aware of it, and wants help that is supportive rather than punitive.

Design implication: because this user is distraction-prone, the app itself must be low-friction, calm, and visually appealing enough to earn repeated attention. UI quality is treated as a retention mechanism, not a cosmetic concern (see ADR-001).
V1 succeeds if it proves the core loop — detect distraction, intervene with CBT, and keep the user coming back — is something a distraction-prone user will actually adopt and sustain.
Intervene at the moment of distraction.
Surface a supportive CBT prompt within the configured threshold of unproductive or not-sure activity, so the user can interrupt an avoidance spiral while it is happening.
Make CBT a sustainable daily habit.
Lower the effort of completing a Procrastination Log enough that the user keeps using it well past the 2–4 week mark where techniques are normally abandoned.
Organize the week and day into a low-friction task flow that opens straight to today’s work, never a dense dashboard.
Give the user an honest view of productive vs. unproductive vs. not-sure time and of the emotional patterns behind their procrastination.
Demonstrate that an active monitoring + CBT-intervention loop changes behavior in a way passive blockers and task managers do not.
Ship a reliable Windows monitoring loop (foreground app detection + Edge URL reading) and a local-first data model that later versions build on without rework.
Prove enough value and retention in V1 to justify the V2–V4 roadmap (timers, journal, goals, and a V4 LLM layer).
Goals are outcomes, not features. Targets for each are proposed as hypotheses in Section 8 (Success metrics).

The following are explicitly out of scope for V1. Each is excluded for a reason, and several are sequenced into later versions (see Section 7.11). Writing them down here prevents scope creep during the build.
A different product category that treats the symptom, not the cause. Possible future integration with Freedom or Cold Turkey; not built in-house.
AI / LLM anywhere in the product
V1 monitoring and intervention are fully algorithmic. The Claude API earns its place in the response/insight layer first, in V4 — never in the monitoring layer.
Edge only in V1. URL reading depends on a per-browser UI Automation approach; other browsers are a later expansion.
Flipping an allowed site to Not-Sure after 5 min of no mouse/keyboard input is deferred to V4. V1 Not-Sure applies only to unlisted apps/sites.
V1 uses a single global allowed/disallowed list. Per-task overrides arrive in V2.
The toggle exists in V1 (default on) but the sub-step engine (min 3 steps, >15-min prompt, per-step time estimates) activates in V2.
Deferred to V2. The only timer in V1 is the 10-minute timer inside a Procrastination Log.
Journal and Calendar are V2; Goal Tracking is V3. (“Goal” still exists as a task category in V1.)
Auto-filling the Task field from the current daily task is V2. V1 is manual entry.
A dashboard is an optional view only. The app opens to the Daily Task view to reduce friction.
Avoided for onboarding friction; pywinauto UI Automation is used instead. Acknowledged as fragile; revisit later as an optional enhancement.
A future iteration, well beyond V1.
5. Core concept — the monitoring + intervention loop
Everything in Momentum orbits a single loop, and that loop is the product’s heartbeat. Everything else is additive.
if Edge, read the active URL
classify the interval as productive / unproductive / not-sure
trigger CBT interventions at configured thresholds

The loop runs every 10 seconds while the app is open. The differentiator is not the tracking itself but what happens at the end of the loop: instead of blocking a site or logging a statistic, Momentum responds to distraction with an empathetic, CBT-grounded intervention.
The two proof-of-concept tests that de-risk this loop have both passed. From the POC README:
Test 1 — Foreground app detection + Edge URL reading.

Node.js reliably identifies the focused Windows app every 10 seconds and filters out minimized and covered windows; a Python sidecar (pywinauto) reads the active Edge URL and passes it back via stdout. Verified across Edge, Slack, File Explorer and Notepad, and across navigation between sites.
A one-time diagnostic that locates the address-bar AutomationId, run only if Edge URL reading breaks after an Edge update. It is a dev tool, not part of the shipping app.
Passing both end-to-end means the handoff —
app detected → URL read → classification triggered
— is proven. That handoff is the core monitoring loop for Momentum V1.
Stories are written from the primary user’s perspective and grouped by area. They describe needs, not UI. Ordered roughly by importance within each group.
As a user, I want to be prompted to list my tasks for the week on my first login of the week, so I commit to what actually matters and push the rest to next week.
As a user, I want to pull a few items from my weekly list into a daily list with one tap, so starting the day is low-friction.
As a user, I want yesterday’s unfinished tasks to roll into today automatically, so nothing quietly falls through the cracks.
As a user, I want to categorize each task (work, personal, hobby, goal), so I can see where my effort is going.
As a user, I want to mark a task complete with a simple strikethrough and check, so finishing feels clean and undramatic.
As a user, I want the app to track which apps and sites are productive, unproductive, or not-sure while it is open, so I have an honest picture of my time without doing any logging myself.
As a user, I want one global list of allowed and disallowed apps and sites, so the rules are simple and predictable.
As a user, I want to decide whether unlisted apps count against me (Strict Mode), so the tracking matches how hard I want to be on myself.
As a user, when I have been unproductive for a couple of minutes, I want a gentle nudge that asks if I’m feeling distracted, so I can catch the spiral early.
As a user, I want that nudge to offer me two supportive next steps — review my risk factors or start a Procrastination Log — rather than just blocking me.
As a user, I want to work through a CBT Procrastination Log one step at a time, so I am never overwhelmed by a wall of form fields.
As a user, I want to name the emotion and challenge the tempting thought behind my avoidance, so I address the feeling, not just the task.
As a user, I want to break the avoided task into 1–3 tiny steps and start a 10-minute timer, so getting started feels possible.
As a user, I want to check my risk factors (sleep, food, water, etc.) and turn one into a recurring daily task, so I address the conditions amplifying my distraction.
As a user, I want a live view of my current session’s productive vs. unproductive vs. not-sure time, so I know how the day is going right now.
As a user, I want a 7-day bar chart of my productivity, so I can see the week’s pattern at a glance.
As a user, I want to be congratulated and nudged to take a healthy break after 50 minutes of focused work, so I sustain focus without burning out.
As a user, I want to revisit my past Procrastination Logs, so I can see what I’ve worked through and notice patterns.
Both P0 and P1 are in V1 scope
— P0 is the minimum viable loop and is built first; P1 completes the V1 experience.
P2 items are explicitly out of V1 scope
and are captured in Section 7.11 so the architecture does not preclude them. Acceptance criteria are the testable definition of done.
Required input on the first login of each week; user can defer non-essential items to next week.
On first login of a new week, the user is prompted to enter that week’s tasks before proceeding.
The user can mark an item to push to next week’s list instead of this week.
The weekly list persists for the week (stored as a weekly task row).
User selects items from the weekly list via an “Add to Daily List” action; low friction.
The user can select one or more weekly items and add them to today’s list in a single action.
After finishing selection, the app moves from the Weekly view to the Daily view.
Daily task rows reference their parent weekly task.
On the first login of a new day, incomplete tasks from the previous day are carried forward.
The app opens to the Daily Task view on first login of the day.
Incomplete tasks from the previous day are automatically added to today.
The user can pull additional items from the weekly list or write in new ones.
Completion is shown as a thin strikethrough plus a green check replacing the open circle. No animation.
Outstanding tasks show an open circle to their right.
Marking complete applies a strikethrough to the text and a green check in the circle.
Completion is stored once on the weekly task row; daily and weekly views both read completion from that single source (no manual syncing).
Each task is assigned a category: Work, Personal, Hobby, or Goal. Required field in V1.
The user must choose one of Work / Personal / Hobby / Goal when creating a task.
The category is stored with the task and available for later filtering/trends.
Optional per task; reminders fire a notification with the task.
A task can optionally be given a due date.
A task can optionally be given a reminder time that fires a Windows notification referencing the task.
Both are optional and absent by default.
Present in Settings, default on. Toggle only in V1; the sub-step engine activates in V2.
A Break Down Mode toggle appears in Settings and defaults to on.
The toggle is functional in the V1 UI but does not yet drive sub-step entry.
Full sub-step behavior (min 3 steps, >15-min split prompt, per-step time estimates) is explicitly deferred to V2.
A single global list of allowed and disallowed apps and websites; applies to all activity by default.
The user can add and remove apps and websites on an allowed list and a disallowed list.
The list is global and applies to all tracking in V1 (per-task overrides are V2).
Global setting that decides how unlisted apps/sites are classified.
With Strict Mode on, an unlisted app/site is classified as Unproductive.
With Strict Mode off, an unlisted app/site is classified as Not-Sure.
The setting is global in V1 (per-task Strict Mode override is V2).
Detect the foreground app every 10 seconds; if it is Edge, read the active URL via the Python sidecar.
Foreground app is sampled every 10 seconds (interval confirmed for V1; 1–10s range under evaluation).
Edge URL is read by the persistent Python sidecar, offset to sample at the midpoint of each Node poll window.
Minimized and covered windows are excluded from classification.
Each interval is recorded as Productive, Unproductive, or Not-Sure.
Productive = actively using an allowed app/site.
Unproductive = actively using a disallowed app/site.
Not-Sure = an app/site on neither list (with Strict Mode off). The Windows desktop/home screen is not polled and never counts.
Unproductive always overrides Productive or Not-Sure.
If multiple Edge windows are open and any is on a disallowed site at poll time, the interval is recorded as Unproductive.
This rule applies regardless of what other windows are showing.
Polling runs whenever the app process is open, including minimized or behind other windows.
Polling continues when the Momentum window is minimized or occluded.
Polling stops only when the user exits the app.
There is no persistent system-tray background process; exit = stop.
Not-Sure applies only to unlisted apps/sites with Strict Mode off.
Inactivity-based Not-Sure (no input on an allowed site → Not-Sure) is NOT implemented in V1.
That behavior is deferred to V4 and must not be built in V1–V3.
Appears after 2 minutes of accumulated unproductive data OR 5 minutes of not-sure data.
Default thresholds: 2 min unproductive, 5 min not-sure.
Both thresholds are configurable in Settings.
The popup offers exactly two next steps and is framed empathetically, not punitively.
Option 1: “View risk factors” → opens the Risk Factors page.
Option 2: “Try a Procrastination Log” → opens a new Procrastination Log (not the logs landing page).
A predefined checklist of conditions that amplify emotion or reduce focus, plus a custom option.
Predefined factors include: eating consistently, water, alcohol, other substances, medication, sleep, exercise, fun activities, social connection, going outside, and sense of accomplishment.
A “write your own” custom option is available (custom entry is P1).
Selecting a factor creates a specially-labelled Procrastination Log.
A log is created labelled “Procrastination - DD/MM/YY hh:mm - lack of [risk factor]”.
It is saved to the Procrastination Logs landing page like any other log.
Selecting a factor creates a daily task to address it, recurring for 2 weeks.
A daily task is auto-created to take action against the chosen factor.
The task recurs daily for 14 days from creation; each morning the app generates that day’s row if one does not already exist.
Note: this auto-recurrence is in V1 scope but flagged for possible revision in V2 (see Open questions).

The Procrastination Log is the most important screen in the app. It must feel supportive, not like a form. Steps are revealed one at a time and the user moves forward sequentially. The full template is defined in Momentum Log Templates.xlsx.
Opening: “Procrastination isn’t a time management problem, it’s an emotional regulation problem.”
A nav-accessible page listing all saved logs, newest first.
Logs are listed sorted by date, labelled “Procrastination - DD/MM/YY hh:mm” (or the “- lack of [risk factor]” variant).
The user can manually create a new log from this page.
Tapping a log opens the full completed log for review.
User types the task they are avoiding; date and time are captured.
The user enters the avoided task as free text.
Date and time are recorded automatically.
Auto-populating the task from the active daily task is deferred to V2.
A single free-text field captures the emotion.
Prompt examples are shown (e.g. overwhelmed, anxious, fear of quality).
Tempting thought, distortion, self-control reframe, and belief ratings.
Tempting Thought captured with % Belief Before (0–100).
User selects a Distortion from the Burns list of 10 (see Appendix B).
Self-Control Thought (reframe) captured with its % Belief (0–100).
% Belief After (0–100) is captured immediately after the reframe, within the same session.
First 1–3 easiest steps (~10 min each) with predictions, a 10-minute timer, and actuals on return.
For each of 1–3 steps: Predicted Difficulty %, predicted time, and Predicted Satisfaction %.
A “Start 10-minute timer” button is available; it fires a Windows notification when it ends.
The log stays open until the user explicitly closes it.
On returning from work, the user fills in Actual Difficulty %, actual time, and Actual Satisfaction % for each step, then closes the log.
Optional free-text reflection; closing quote shown.
A free-text Takeaways field is presented and is optional.
The closing quote is shown: “The dread is always worse than the actual doing of the task.”
On close, the completed log is saved to the landing page with its timestamp.
Steps are displayed individually to avoid overwhelming the user.
Only the current step is shown; the user advances forward through steps.
The screen reads as supportive guidance, not a dense multi-field form.
A 7-day bar chart for the current week.
A bar chart shows the current week (7 days).
Each day’s bar shows productive / unproductive / not-sure totals.
Minute-level roll-ups are computed at query time from session data.
Live productive vs. unproductive vs. not-sure totals for the active session.
The view shows running totals for the three data types for the current session.
Totals update as the monitoring loop records intervals.
Congratulatory notification after 50 continuous minutes of productive data, with healthy break suggestions.
Triggers after 50 continuous minutes of productive data.
Fires a Windows notification with a congratulatory message.
Suggests healthy breaks (e.g. a walk with no music, stretching, water or a healthy snack, fresh air and sunshine, chatting with a friend, gratitude, breathing/meditation).
Pomodoro-based break logic is V2 and replaces this when active.
The app opens to the Daily Task view, never the dashboard.
On launch (after any weekly/daily prompts), the Daily Task view is shown.
A dashboard, if present, is an optional view only and is not the default.
Configurable popup thresholds and the Break Down Mode toggle.
The unproductive and not-sure popup thresholds are editable (defaults 2 min / 5 min).
The Break Down Mode toggle is present and defaults to on.
Core V1 pages are reachable from the main navigation, with Settings bottom-left.
Nav exposes at minimum: Weekly Tasks, Daily Tasks, Allow/Disallow Apps & Sites, Risk Factors, Procrastination Logs, Productivity Trends, Current Session.
A Settings entry point sits in the bottom-left corner.
Pages not in V1 scope (Calendar, Goal Tracking, Journal, Dashboard) are not required.
Local-first storage; one row per contiguous session, not per poll.
Monitoring data is held in memory and written to SQLite on app-switch (close outgoing session, open new one).
A periodic safety flush (every 60–120s) protects against crashes.
Timestamps are stored as Unix seconds (INTEGER).
No cloud dependency in V1. Full schema is defined in CLAUDE.md (see Appendix A).
7.11 Future considerations (P2 — out of V1 scope)
These are not built in V1. They are recorded so V1 architectural choices (the session-based SQLite model, the Node response layer, the React UI) do not make them expensive later.
Per-task allowed/disallowed and Strict Mode overrides.
Full Break Down Mode: sub-steps (min 3), a prompt to subdivide any step over 15 minutes, and per-step time estimates.
Countdown timer (any duration) and Pomodoro timer (25/5, 50/10, or custom; increments only on productive data; break doubles every 2 consecutive Pomodoros).
Journal (thought records, strong-emotion entries, gratitude logs) and a Calendar view (daily/weekly history and upcoming).
Break notifier integrates with Pomodoro; Procrastination Log auto-populates its Task field from the active daily task.
Goal tracking (short/long-term goals, objectives, milestones with dates, reminders).
Optional dashboard UI (still not the default startup view).
Daily usage data: daily-use flag, total days used, consecutive-day streak, monthly totals; productivity trends extended beyond the current week.

LLM integration via the official Anthropic Claude JS SDK in the response/insight layer (never monitoring): AI-assisted Procrastination Logs, AI risk-factor pattern analysis, AI task breakdown, and AI daily/weekly review (mirroring the /good-morning and /lets-go-home flows). Local/offline LLM (e.g. Ollama) evaluated as an option.
Premium UI polish: full Tailwind + Framer Motion + component-library treatment, Figma handoff, consistent motion and accessible color.

Windows release & quality: regression testing of the monitoring loop, security review (SQLite, IPC, sidecar stdout), AutomationId auto-diagnostic/recovery, performance audit, installer + auto-updater, and distribution.
There is no live usage data yet, so every target below is a

, not a committed number. Measurement is local-first and privacy-respecting (no telemetry assumed in V1); instrumentation approach is an open question for the data owner. The single most important question V1 must answer is the retention one: does active support carry the user past the 2–4 week cliff where CBT habits are normally abandoned?
Sustained daily engagement is the precondition for habit formation.
≥60% of “Feeling Distracted?” popups acted on (vs. dismissed)
Tests whether the in-the-moment nudge is welcome rather than ignored.
≥40% of started logs completed through Step 5
The log is the core CBT mechanism; abandonment signals too much friction.
Avg. ≥20-point drop from % Belief Before to % Belief After
Direct evidence the reframe is doing cognitive work.
Shows the task flow is realistic, not aspirational overload.
≥50% still active at day 30; ≥30% at day 60
The core thesis of the entire product. This is the metric that justifies V2–V4.
Upward trend over the first 8 weeks
Downward trend over the first 8 weeks
The lived outcome the user actually wants.
User reports CBT habit feels sustainable at week 8
Qualitative confirmation of the central hypothesis.
Genuinely unresolved items, tagged by who should answer and whether they block the V1 build.
Should inspect-edge-tree.py logic be bundled into the app as an auto-diagnostic that detects and recovers from Edge AutomationId changes without an app update?
Final polling interval within the confirmed 1–10s range — keep 10s, or tune down based on feel?
Risk Factors auto-recurring daily task (14 days): keep as specified for V1, or revise the recurrence model?
How should the Procrastination Log render “one step at a time” so it feels supportive rather than like a wizard/form?
What exactly does the Break Down Mode toggle do in the V1 UI while its sub-step engine is inert?
Metric instrumentation: how are leading/lagging metrics captured locally given the no-telemetry, privacy-first framing?
On app startup, verify the Edge address bar is reachable and surface a clear error if not — what is the fallback UX?

Momentum is a solo, AI-assisted build (Claude Code for the app, Claude Design for UI, Cowork for documentation). There are no contractual deadlines; sequencing is driven by de-risking the core loop first.
Both POC tests pass (foreground detection + Edge URL reading; AutomationId diagnostic).
Framework decided: Electron (Node.js + TypeScript + React) per ADR-001.
Next step: scaffold the Electron app.
Electron scaffold; SQLite schema and session-write model; port the POC monitoring loop (Node detection + Python sidecar) into the app.
Global allow/disallow + Strict Mode; classification + priority rule; Current Session view; “Feeling Distracted?” popup; the 5-step Procrastination Log and its landing page; Risk Factors.
Weekly/daily tasks, new-day carryover, categories, completion; default startup to Daily Tasks; core navigation; Settings.
Productivity Trends 7-day chart; Break Notifier; due dates & reminders; configurable thresholds; Break Down Mode toggle.
Review monitoring performance (Chromium overhead) at end of V1; this is the trigger condition for any future Tauri migration evaluation (ADR-001).
V1 stores everything locally in SQLite. Timestamps are Unix seconds. The authoritative schema is maintained in CLAUDE.md and is not duplicated here to avoid drift; the tables are:
— one row per contiguous monitoring session (app, url, classification 1/2/3, start/end, total seconds).
— weekly and daily tasks; completion stored only on the weekly row, with daily rows referencing it via parent_task_id.
— CBT thought records (emotion, tempting thought, distortion, belief ratings, takeaways, optional risk_factor).
— 1–3 task-breakdown steps per log, with predicted vs. actual difficulty/time/satisfaction.
— recurring daily tasks auto-created from the Risk Factors page (recur_until = 14 days from creation).
The Distortion field in the Procrastination Log is selected from the Burns list of ten:
Jumping to conclusions (mind reading / fortune telling)
The Risk Factors page offers these predefined factors (plus a custom option):
Connecting with someone you care about
End of document — Momentum PRD V1 (Draft 1.0, 26 May 2026).
